title: The Faithful Parent 6: Parenting from Conception
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 01-21-24 - The Faithful Parent 6 - Parenting from Conception - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Parenting Begins at Conception, Not at Birth
subpoints: The baby in the womb is a full human being and a full person from conception
Peter Singer's view: personhood arrives at age 8 — same logic as American slavery
Dehumanizing language (fetus, embryo) subtly distances us from recognizing the baby as a person

outline.title: Why Abortion Is Wrong: The Image of God (Genesis 9:1–7)
subpoints: Genesis 9 re-establishes the creation mandate after the flood — God is for life
Verse 6: innocent life must not be taken because man is made in God's image
Calvin: 'No one can injure his brother without wounding God himself'
Animal life is not equivalent to human life — man holds dominion
The image of God is borne by all humanity without exception (James 3:9; Acts 17:26)

outline.title: What the Mosaic Law Teaches About Life in the Womb
subpoints: Exodus 21:22–23: if men fighting accidentally harm a pregnant woman, criminal penalties apply to both mother and child
The baby in the womb is protected as a citizen by Old Testament criminal law
Old Testament prophets treat killing the unborn as a sign of divine judgment on Israel's enemies

outline.title: Scripture Addresses the Baby as a Person from Conception
subpoints: Psalm 51:5 — David was sinful from the moment of conception, implying personhood at that point
Psalm 139:13–16 — 'I' was woven together in the womb; David identifies himself with the person in the womb
Jeremiah 1:5 — known and set apart before birth
John the Baptist worshipped Christ while still in the womb

outline.title: Current Context and Application
subpoints: Washington State HB 1954: protects abortion providers and makes Washington a sanctuary for those violating other states' pro-life laws
The Dobbs decision did not end the battle for the sanctity of life
The church must also care for vulnerable mothers — a distinct obligation, not a counter-argument
Racism follows the same root logic as abortion: attributing lesser personhood to some human beings
Book recommendation: Ryan T. Anderson and Alexandra DeSanctis, Tearing Us Apart

summary: Pastor Tito Lyro teaches that parenting begins at conception, establishing from Genesis 9, Exodus 21, and Psalms 51 and 139 that the baby in the womb is a full human being made in the image of God whose life is sacred from the moment of conception. He refutes Peter Singer's view that personhood comes later, applies the Mosaic law's criminal protection of unborn life, and addresses the current political climate in Washington State, connecting the sanctity of life to the broader theme of parenting.
transcript:
## Introduction: Parenting Begins at Conception

This is our sixth lesson in the series on the faithful parent. We have covered an introduction to what the Bible generally teaches about raising children and the duties of parents. Then we looked at the infant year, the toddler years, an overall introduction to the theology of spanking, the preschool years, and last week the elementary school years.

Today, rather than moving to teenagers as you might expect, I want to emphasize something foundational: parenting begins at conception — not at birth. From the moment a baby is conceived, there is a mother, a father, and grandparents. That is one of the reasons why I think it is so important, and this is a personal opinion, that we learn the gender of the baby and name them sooner rather than later in the womb. So we can begin referring to the baby as Abigail, not as "the baby," or as "it." This helps us think of the child as a full human being in the womb.

The baby growing in the womb is a full human being — not only that, but also a full person.

## The Distinction Between Human Being and Person

Some of you may think: aren't those the same thing? Sadly, that is no longer the broadly accepted view in philosophy. There is now a deliberate divorcing of being a human being from being a person. This came about as technology advanced to the point where no one could deny that what grows in the womb is a human being. In response, some argued: yes, it is a human being, but not yet a person — and personhood, they said, is gained later in life.

Peter Singer, a prominent bioethicist at Princeton University, suggests that a child does not become a person until at least age eight. On his view, anything done to a child before that point is not done against a person and is not covered by law.

Do you know another institution in American history that followed the same reasoning? Slavery. The Supreme Court ruled that for the purposes of voting, African Americans were not persons. The logic is identical: denying full personhood to human beings in order to justify treating them as less than human.

We must also be careful about the language we allow to creep into our vocabulary — even in the church. We sometimes refer to the baby using clinical terms like "fetus" (which is simply the Latin word for "baby"), "embryo," or even "zygote." While these terms sound scientific, they have the effect of distancing us from recognizing that we are talking about a baby. In reality, the baby in the womb is a little human being bearing the image of God.

## Why Abortion Is Wrong: The Image of God (Genesis 9:1–7)

Why is abortion wrong? Not because the Constitution says so. Not because the Supreme Court has said so. Abortion is wrong because God in His Word says so.

Let us look at Genesis 9:1–7. This is the covenant with Noah. The flood has ended. God is recreating humanity and entering into covenant with Noah as the representative of all creation:

> "So God blessed Noah and his sons, and said to them: 'Be fruitful and multiply, and fill the earth. And the fear of you and the dread of you shall be on every beast of the earth... Whoever sheds man's blood, by man his blood shall be shed; for in the image of God He made man.'" (Genesis 9:1–2, 6)

Notice how God sandwiches this passage with the command to be fruitful and multiply — repeating the creation mandate from Genesis 1 and 2. God is recreating the earth after the flood and reaffirming that He is the God of life, not death.

Verse 6 gives us the foundation: it is forbidden to take innocent human life because man is made in the image of God. John Calvin commenting on this says: "No one can be injurious to his brother without wounding God himself, because we bear the image of God."

### What the Image of God Means

The image of God is something all of humanity bears — and continues to bear. James 3:9 tells us that with our tongues we curse "man, who has been made in the likeness of God." The perfect tense indicates it was made in the past and continues. Acts 17:26 tells us that God has made "from one blood every nation of men." Every ethnicity, however different outwardly, shares the same origin and the same image-bearing dignity.

To be made in the image of God is to be made a spiritual being — one capable of relating to God not merely as creature to Creator, but as children to Father. It includes the possession of an eternal soul and the ability to know and be known by God.

This is why racism and abortion share the same sinful root: both attribute lesser worth or personhood to some human beings.

## The Mosaic Law's Protection of Life in the Womb

The Old Testament does not leave us without direct guidance. Exodus 21:22–23 gives us a case law:

> "If men fight and hurt a woman with child, so that she gives birth prematurely, yet no harm follows, he shall surely be punished accordingly as the woman's husband imposes on him... But if any harm follows, then you shall give life for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth..." (Exodus 21:22–23)

The scenario is clear: two men fighting accidentally strike a pregnant woman. If no harm comes to either mother or child, there is a fine. If harm comes — to either the mother or the baby — criminal punishment follows, up to and including capital punishment. The baby in the womb is protected as a citizen under criminal law. To claim the Old Testament permitted abortion is to not actually read the Old Testament.

## Scripture Addresses the Baby as a Person from Conception

Scripture repeatedly speaks of the baby in the womb as a person:

- **Psalm 51:5**: "Surely I was sinful at birth, sinful from the time my mother conceived me." David identifies himself — the full person — with the moment of conception.
- **Psalm 139:13–16**: "For You formed my inward parts; You covered me in my mother's womb... I will praise You, for I am fearfully and wonderfully made." The "I" who speaks is identified with the being formed in the womb.
- **Jeremiah 1:5**: "Before I formed you in the womb I knew you; before you were born I sanctified you."
- John the Baptist, while still in the womb, leaped for joy in the presence of his Savior.

The conclusion is inescapable: the baby in the womb is a human being, made from conception in the image of God, whose life is sacred in God's sight.

## Application: The Battle for Life Is Not Over

Some assume that since the Dobbs decision overturned Roe v. Wade, the battle is won. It is not. Consider Washington State House Bill 1954, introduced on the very first day of the 2024 legislative session. Titled "Harmonizing Language Relating to Reproductive Health Care Services," it adds protections for medical providers performing abortions and makes Washington a sanctuary state for providers who violate pro-life laws in other states. The value of life has reached an all-time low in the Western world.

Some will say: if you care so much about the baby, why don't you care about the mother? We should — and we must. But these are two separate issues. The church's possible failure to care for mothers does not justify killing babies. Two wrongs do not make a right.

For further reading on natural law arguments for life: Ryan T. Anderson and Alexandra DeSanctis, *Tearing Us Apart: How Abortion Harms Everything and Solves Nothing*.

Next week we will look at Proverbs 24:11–12 — what we are called to do in the face of this reality — and then we will turn to parenting teenagers.
